coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen, cobalt-codoped carbon electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction using soy milk and cobalt salts as precursors
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Li
Zhang, Lingling
Bai, Lu
Han, Lei
Dong, Shaojun
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen and cobalt codoped carbon electrocatalyst using soy milk and cobalt salts as precursors has been examined for its electrocatalytic activity toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Carbon dots (C-d) were first synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of soy milk. Then, cobalt (II) nitrate was incorporated with C-d via heat treatment to generate nitrogen and cobalt codoped carbon composites (Co/C). When polypyrrole was introduced in the synthetic process, carbon composites with higher nitrogen and cobalt contents (Co/N/C) than Co/C were obtained, which exhibited high electrocatalytic activity, excellent methanol crossover effect, and good stability toward ORR in alkaline solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oxygen reduction reaction
N, Co-codoped carbon electrocatalyst
Soy milk
Cobalt salts
Carbon dots
Polypyrrole

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) CVs of the Co/N/C electrode in N2- and O2-saturated 0.1M KOH at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. (B) RRDE voltammograms of the Co/N/C, Co/C, C-d, ppy, Pt/C electrodes, and GCE in an O2-saturated 0.1M KOH solution at a scan rate of 10mVs−1 and a rotation rate of 1600rpm and (C) those of corresponding H2O2 products at the Pt ring electrodes in the RRDE system. (D) Electron number (n) obtained from RRDE tests. (E) RDE voltammograms of Co/N/C electrode in O2-saturated 0.1M KOH with various rotation rates at a scan rate of 10mVs−1. (F) Koutecky–Levich plots of Co/N/C electrode at different potential. Inset: the electron transfer number n.
XPS survey spectra of Co/C (A) and Co/N/C (B); XPS N1s spectra of Co/C (C) and Co/N/C (D); and XPS Co2p spectra of Co/C (E) and Co/N/C (F). SEM image (G) and X-ray diffractogram (H) of Co/N/C.

CVs of Co/N/C (A) and Pt/C (B) before and after stability test (2000cycles in oxygen saturated 0.1M KOH at a scan rate of 50mV/s). CVs of Co/N/C (C) and Pt/C (D) with and without 1M methanol in oxygen saturated 0.1M KOH at a scan rate of 50mV/s.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen, cobalt-codoped carbon electrocatalyst for oxygen reduction reaction using soy milk and cobalt salts as precursors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen and cobalt codoped carbon electrocatalyst using soy milk and cobalt salts as precursors has been examined for its electrocatalytic activity toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). Carbon dots (C-d) were first synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of soy milk. Then, cobalt (II) nitrate was incorporated with C-d via heat treatment to generate nitrogen and cobalt codoped carbon composites (Co/C). When polypyrrole was introduced in the synthetic process, carbon composites with higher nitrogen and cobalt contents (Co/N/C) than Co/C were obtained, which exhibited high electrocatalytic activity, excellent methanol crossover effect, and good stability toward ORR in alkaline solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: For fuel cell, the cathode catalyst involving the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), plays a vital role in determining the fuel cell's performance, such as the power output, open circuit potential, cycling life and so forth [1–3]. Pt and its alloys are well known as active and efficient catalysts for ORR, but their high cost, limited supply, and weak durability severely hinder their applicability to broad commercialization [4]. Therefore, the development of non-precious catalysts with high ORR activity and high durability and much lower cost is highly desirable [5].

N-doped carbon materials have been considered to be alternative electrocatalysts for ORR due to their high catalytic activity and durability toward ORR [6–9]. Our group's previous works presented that catalysts with Fe (or Co) coordinated to heterocyclic N possess high ORR activity [10–16]. Moreover, researchers have shown that modifying N-doped carbon materials with Fe (or Co) can enhance their electrocatalytic activity for ORR, by using right precursors and heat-treatment conditions [17–23]. Soy milk abounds with carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements, we expected this natural source could provide a promising precursor to synthesize advantageous carbon nanomaterials. And soy milk could be gotten much more easily than some reported carbon materials such as carbon nanotube and graphene. Herein, we developed a low cost strategy toward nitrogen and cobalt codoped carbon composites (Co/C) by using soy milk and cobalt salts. Then carbon composites with higher nitrogen and cobalt contents (Co/N/C) with the aid of polypyrrole (ppy) were obtained. The oxygen reduction activities and surface properties were characterized by cyclic voltammetry (CV), rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE), rotating disk electrode (RDE), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and scanning electron microscope (SEM).

Carbon-supported Pt catalyst (20wt.%, Pt/C) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Pyrrole (99%) and Nafion (5wt.% in a mixture of lower aliphatic alcohols and water) were all purchased from Aldrich Chemistry Co. Ltd. Co(NO3)2·6H2O was obtained from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (>18MΩcm).

A XL30 ESEM scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to determine the morphology. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried on an ESCALAB MK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (VG Scientific, UK). Electrochemical measurements were performed with a CH 660 electrochemical analyzer (CH Instruments, Inc., USA). A conventional three-electrode cell was used, including an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode as the reference electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 5mm in diameter) as the working electrode. Rotating disk electrode (RDE) and rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) measurements were carried out on a Princeton Applied Research Model 636 and the CH 660 electrochemical analyzer.

Carbon dots (C-d) were synthesized from soy milk according to the literature with a little modification [24]. The typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the as-prepared carbon dots shows that their diameters are mainly about 3nm. The specific surface area of carbon dot was calculated to be 2nm2/nm3. Then the mixture solution of C-d (100mg) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (35mg) was kept stirring at 100°C until the water completely evaporated and then pyrolysed at 800°C for 2h and the pyrolysis products were denoted as Co/C.

40mL FeCl3·6H2O (0.78g) solution was added into the mixed solution of C-d (200mg) and pyrrole (80μL) as an oxidant for the polymerization of pyrrole monomer [25], followed by vigorous stirring for 5h. After polymerization, the composites were collected by filtration and washed thoroughly with a large amount of water and subsequently with ethanol and dried. The obtained material (100mg) was added into a solution of Co(NO3)2·6H2O (30mg in 25mL of water). The mixture was kept stirring at 100°C until the water completely evaporated and then pyrolysed at 800°C for 2h, and the product was denoted as Co/N/C.

GCE was sequentially polished by using 1.0 and 0.3μm alumina slurry and then washed ultrasonically in water and ethanol, respectively. 2mg of the total catalyst and 20μL Nafion solution (5wt.%) were dispersed in 1mL of ethanol in water (1:3v/v) solution by sonication at least for 30min. 10μL of the prepared slurry was dropped on the pretreated GCE surface and dried at the room temperature. Catalyst loading was 0.1mgcm−2 for all samples.

The electrocatalytic activity of Co/N/C electrode for ORR was first examined in a 0.1M KOH solution saturated with N2 or O2 by cyclic voltammetry (CV) (Fig. 1A); featureless voltammetric current within the potential range of −1.0 to 0.2V was observed for Co/N/C electrode in the N2-saturated solution. In contrast, when the electrolyte was saturated with O2, a well-defined cathodic peak centered at −0.21V was detected, suggesting a pronounced electrocatalytic activity for ORR.

The Co/N/C electrode was then evaluated using the RRDE system. Fig. 1B shows disk polarization curves representing ORR activities of Co/N/C, Co/C, C-d, ppy, Pt/C electrodes and GCE, and the corresponding amperometric responses of these electrodes for the oxidation of hydrogen peroxide ions (HO2 −) measured with a Pt ring electrode at the potential of 0.50V are included in Fig. 1C. Like the Pt/C electrode, the Co/N/C electrode exhibited a one-step process for the ORR with the onset potential of −0.005V (Fig. 1B) and a low ring current (Fig. 1C), which suggests a four-electron pathway for the ORR at the Co/N/C electrode. However, the Co/C electrode exhibited lower onset ORR potential (−0.09V) and lower plateau current than Co/N/C electrode. In addition, the C-d electrode showed a two-step process for ORR with the onset potential of about −0.12V and −0.60V, respectively, consistent with previous reports [24,26].

The electron transfer number per oxygen molecule (n) in ORR can be calculated using the following equation: n =4ID /(ID
+ IR /N) [27,28], where ID , IR and N are disk current, ring current, and collection efficiency (0.21), respectively. Fig. 1D shows n value involved in the ORR of the Co/N/C, Co/C, C-d, and Pt/C electrodes. The electron transfer number at Co/N/C electrode was around 3.8 in the potential range from −0.3V to −0.8V. The percentage of H2O2 produced in ORR can be calculated using the following equation: H2O2 (%)=100(4− n)/2 [27,28], which were calculated to be lower than 10% over the potential range from −0.3 to −0.8V. These values indicate that ORR at the Co/N/C electrode favors a four-electron pathway. RDE measurements show that the limiting current density on Co/N/C increases with increasing rotation rate (from 250rpm to 2000rpm) (Fig. 1E). The number of electrons (n) involved per O2 in the ORR on Co/N/C was determined by the Koutechy–Levich equation: 1/j=1/jk +1/jL =1/jk +(0.62nFAD2/3C0ν−1/6ω1/2)−1, where F is the Faraday constant (F=96485 C mol−1), A is the geometric area of RDE, D is the diffusion coefficient of O2 in 0.1M KOH electrolyte (1.9×10−5 cm2 s−1), C0 is the bulk concentration of O2 (1.2×10−6 molcm−3), ν is the kinetic viscosity of the electrolyte (0.01cm2 s−1), and ω is the angular velocity of the disk. The calculated number of electrons involved in the reduction of the oxygen from Koutecky–Levich plots (Fig. 1F) is close to those obtained from RRDE.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurements were then carried out to probe the chemical composition and contents of N and Co in Co/N/C (Fig. 2 ). N and Co contents of Co/N/C are increased to 4.78% and 1.02%, compared to those of Co/C samples, respectively. Ppy is largely decomposed by heat-treatment at 800°C and smaller nitrogen-containing molecules could be formed and evaporated [29], and Co nanoparticles associated with NC moieties that originate from the PPy structures left [30], which could result in increased atomic% of Co. The complex N1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three different peaks, the nitrogen state N1 (located at 397.5eV) could be speculated to be the CoN bond, and the nitrogen states N2 (located at 399.8eV) and N3 (located at 401.1eV) could be attributed to the pyrrolic N bonding with hydrogen (HN type), and graphitic N, respectively [20,21]. Compared to Co/C, the contents of pyrrolic N in Co/N/C are increased. Based on the high ORR activity of Co/N/C it is reasonable to assume that N and Co play important roles for the enhanced activity in alkaline solution. Also, our work highlights previous conclusion that pyridinic N alone may be not effective for the 4-electron ORR [31–33]. Typical SEM image (Fig. 2G), in association with XRD (as shown in Fig. 2H), shows that Co particles were formed after the thermal decomposition of cobalt (II) salts under high temperature.

In addition, the durability tests revealed that the Co/N/C had a high cycling stability and tolerance to the crossover effect. As shown in Fig. 3A, after 2000 consecutive CV cycles in O2-saturated KOH, the CV of Co/N/C electrode did not show a significant change, which is in contrast to the decrease of ORR activity at the Pt/C electrode (Fig. 3B). Moreover, methanol, a common fuel for fuel cells, was added to the O2-saturated KOH to examine the resistance of Co/N/C electrode to the crossover effect. As shown in Fig. 3C, the CV of Co/N/C electrode in the presence of 1M methanol did not change much, indicating that methanol did not interfere with the ORR at the cathode. However, oxidation of the methanol occurred on the Pt/C electrode (Fig. 3D), which greatly compromised the ORR efficiency. From these electrochemical characterizations, it can be seen that Co/N/C shows superior methanol tolerance and stability for the oxygen reduction in alkaline medium than Pt/C, but the onset potential and current density of Co/N/C is not as good as commercial Pt/C.

In summary, we developed a cost-effective synthesis of N, Co-codoped carbon electrocatalyst (Co/C) for ORR using soy milk and cobalt salts as precursors. Although Co/C have catalytic activity for ORR, Co/N/C with higher nitrogen and cobalt contents could be obtained when polypyrrole was introduced in the synthetic process, and the electrochemical tests showed that the Co/N/C has high ORR activity in alkaline solutions, favors a four-electron pathway, and exceeds Pt/C in stability and anticrossover property. The study reveals that the enhanced proportions of N and Co are the main reasons for the enhanced catalytic activity towards ORR.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (200935003, 21075116) and 973 projects (2011CB911002, 2010CB933603).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Journal of the Iranian Chemical Society
The Journal of Physical Chemistry B
Journal of Thermal Analysis and Calorimetry
